subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Two bodies $A$ and $B$ of mass $m$ and $2 m$ respectively are placed on a smooth floor. They are connected by a spring of negligible mass. $A$ third body $C$ of mass $m$ is placed on the floor. The body $C$ moves with a velocity $v_0$ along the line joining $A$ and $B$ and collides elastically with $A$. At a certain time after the collision it is found that the instantaneous velocities of $A$ and $B$ are same and the compression of the spring is $x_0$. The spring constant $k$ will be
question: Two bodies $A$ and $B$ of mass $m$ and $2 m$ respectively are placed on a smooth floor. They are connected by a spring of negligible mass. $A$ third body $C$ of mass $m$ is placed on the floor. The body $C$ moves with a velocity $v_0$ along the line joining $A$ and $B$ and collides elastically with $A$. At a certain time after the collision it is found that the instantaneous velocities of $A$ and $B$ are same and the compression of the spring is $x_0$. The spring constant $k$ will be
solution: Initial momentum of the system block $(C)$ $=m v_0$. After striking with $A$, the block $C$ comes to rest and now both block A and B moves with velocity $\mathrm{v}$ when compression in spring is $x_0$. By the law of conservation of linear momentum $$ m v_0=(m+2 m) v \Rightarrow v=\frac{v_0}{3} $$ By the law of conservation of energy K.E. of block $C=$ K.E. of system + P.E. of system $$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} m v_0^2=\frac{1}{2}(3 m)\left(\frac{v_0}{3}\right)^2+\frac{1}{2} k x_0^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} m v_0^2=\frac{1}{6} m v_0^2+\frac{1}{2} k x_0^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} k x_0^2=\frac{1}{2} m v_0^2-\frac{1}{6} m v_0^2=\frac{m v_0^2}{3} \\ & \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2} k x_0^2=\frac{1}{2} m v_0^2-\frac{1}{6} m v_0^2=\frac{m v_0^2}{3} \\ & \therefore \quad k=\frac{2}{3} m\left(\frac{v_0}{x_0}\right)^2 \end{aligned} $$

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A bullet of 10 g, moving with velocity v, collides head-on with the stationary bob of a pendulum and recoils with velocity 100 m s-1. The length of the pendulum is 0.5 m and mass of the bob is 1 kg. The minimum value of v in m s-1, so that the pendulum describes a circle. (Assume the string to be inextensible and g=10 m s-2 )
question: A bullet of 10 g, moving with velocity v, collides head-on with the stationary bob of a pendulum and recoils with velocity 100 m s-1. The length of the pendulum is 0.5 m and mass of the bob is 1 kg. The minimum value of v in m s-1, so that the pendulum describes a circle. (Assume the string to be inextensible and g=10 m s-2 )
solution:
Final velocity of system (bullet + mass) should be =5gr

Now M5gr=m(v+100)

thus v=Mm5gr-100=500-100=400 m s-1

v=400 m s-1

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Two blocks of masses $2$ kg and $1$ kg respectively, are tied to the ends of a string which passes over a light frictionless pulley as shown in the figure below. The masses are held at rest at the same horizontal level and then released. The distance traversed by the centre of mass in $2$ s is _______ m. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
question: Two blocks of masses $2$ kg and $1$ kg respectively, are tied to the ends of a string which passes over a light frictionless pulley as shown in the figure below. The masses are held at rest at the same horizontal level and then released. The distance traversed by the centre of mass in $2$ s is _______ m. (Take $g = 10$ m/s$^2$)
solution:
The acceleration of the blocks in the Atwood machine is given by:

$a = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{m_1 + m_2} g$

Substituting the given values ($m_1 = 2$ kg, $m_2 = 1$ kg):

$a = \dfrac{2 - 1}{2 + 1} g = \dfrac{g}{3}$

Taking the downward direction as positive, the acceleration of the $2$ kg block is $a_1 = \dfrac{g}{3}$ and the acceleration of the $1$ kg block is $a_2 = -\dfrac{g}{3}$.

The acceleration of the centre of mass is:

$a_{cm} = \dfrac{m_1 a_1 + m_2 a_2}{m_1 + m_2}$

$a_{cm} = \dfrac{2 \left(\dfrac{g}{3}\right) + 1 \left(-\dfrac{g}{3}\right)}{2 + 1} = \dfrac{\dfrac{g}{3}}{3} = \dfrac{g}{9}$

Since the system is released from rest, the initial velocity of the centre of mass is zero.

The distance traversed by the centre of mass in $t = 2$ s is:

$S_{cm} = \dfrac{1}{2} a_{cm} t^2$

$S_{cm} = \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{10}{9}\right) (2)^2 = \dfrac{20}{9} \approx 2.22 \text{ m}$

Answer: $2.22$

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> balls each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> moving with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> simultaneously strike a wall normally and reflected back with same speed, in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The total force exerted by the balls on the wall is
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> balls each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> moving with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>v</mi></math> simultaneously strike a wall normally and reflected back with same speed, in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The total force exerted by the balls on the wall is
solution:
.

Force exerted by each ball is F=∆p∆t, here, p is momentum and t is time.

So, for one ball F=mv-m-vt=2mvt

Now, total force exerted by the balls on the wall is Fnet=100×F=200mvt.

A mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> moving with velocity <math><mi>v</mi></math> making angle <math><mi>θ</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> to the rod’s long axis collides with one end of the rod and sticks to it. The angular speed of the rod-mass system just after the collision is:
solution:
Loi=Lof
mV2×L2=4mL212+mL24×ω
ω=6V72L=32V7L

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Three objects A, B and C are kept in a straight line on a frictionless horizontal surface. The masses of A, B and C are m, 2m and 2m respectively. A moves towards B with a speed of 9 m s-1 and makes an elastic collision with it. Thereafter B makes a completely inelastic collision with C. All motions occur along the same straight line. The final speed of C is :
question: Three objects A, B and C are kept in a straight line on a frictionless horizontal surface. The masses of A, B and C are m, 2m and 2m respectively. A moves towards B with a speed of 9 m s-1 and makes an elastic collision with it. Thereafter B makes a completely inelastic collision with C. All motions occur along the same straight line. The final speed of C is :
solution:
Collision between A and B

m×9=mv1+2 mv2 (from momentum conservation)
e=1=v2-v19
⇒v2=6 m sec-1, v1=-3 m sec-1
Collision between B and C

2 m×6=4mv (from momentum conservation)
v=3 m s-1

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> moving with an unknown velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mi> </mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi></math> undergoes a collinear collision with a body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> moving with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi> </mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> After the collision, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> move with velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0.5</mn><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> then <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is:
question: A body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> moving with an unknown velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mi> </mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi></math> undergoes a collinear collision with a body of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> moving with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi> </mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> After the collision, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> move with velocities of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0.5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mi>m</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>0.5</mn><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> then <math><msub><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> is:
solution:
From the conservation of linear momentum,
m1v1+m2v2=m1v3+m2v4
m1v1+0.5 m1 v2=m10.5 v1+0.5 m1 v4
0.5 v1+0.5 v2=0.5 v4
v4=v1+v2
v1=v4-v2

q: Four particles A, B, C  and D with masses mA=m,mB=2m,mC=3m  and mD=4m are at the corners of a square. They have accelerations of equal magnitude with directions as shown. The acceleration of the centre of mass of the particles is:
question: Four particles A, B, C  and D with masses mA=m,mB=2m,mC=3m  and mD=4m are at the corners of a square. They have accelerations of equal magnitude with directions as shown. The acceleration of the centre of mass of the particles is:
solution:
a→A=-ai^
a→B=aj^
a→C=ai^
a→D=-aj^
a→cm=maa→a+mba→b+mca→c+mda→dma+mb+mc+md
a→cm=-mai^+2maj^+3mai^-4maj^10m

=2mai^-2maj^10,=a5i^-a5j^=a5i^-j^

q: A nucleus disintegrates into two smaller parts, which have their velocities in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. The ratio of their nuclear sizes will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup></math>. The value of ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>’ is:
question: A nucleus disintegrates into two smaller parts, which have their velocities in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. The ratio of their nuclear sizes will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></msup></math>. The value of ‘<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>’ is:
solution:
Given here, v1v2=32

Using conservation of momentum during disintegration, we have

m1v1=m2v2⇒m1 m2=23

Since, m=43πR3ρ 

We have m∝R3  ∵nuclear mass density, ρ=constant

m1m2=R1R23

⇒R1R2=2313

So, x=2.

question: Three point particles of masses 1.0 kg, 1.5 kg and 2.5 kg are placed at three corners of a right angle triangle of sides 4.0 cm, 3.0 cm and 5.0 cm as shown in the figure. The centre of mass of the system is at a point:
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.6</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> right and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> mass.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> right and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> mass.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> right and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> mass.

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.9</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> right and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2.0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> above <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> mass.
solution:
Take 1 kg mass at origin

Xcm=1×0+1.5×3+2.5×05=0.9 cm
Ycm=1×0+1.5×0+2.5×45=2 cm

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> hits the wall with its initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and bounces back without changing its initial speed. If the force applied by the wall on the ball during the contact is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. calculate the time duration of the contact of ball with the wall.
question: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> hits the wall with its initial speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and bounces back without changing its initial speed. If the force applied by the wall on the ball during the contact is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>. calculate the time duration of the contact of ball with the wall.
solution:
Given: m=1.5 kg & u=12 m s-1

Change in the momentum of the ball after the collision=2mv

=2×1.5×12

=36 N s

As the force applied during collision is equal to 100 N and if t is the duration of collision, so

100×t=Δp

⇒t=36100 s

t=36×10-2 s=0.036 s

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: It is found that if a neutron suffers an elastic collinear collision with a deuterium at rest, the fractional loss of its energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></msub></math>, while for its similar collision with a carbon nucleus at rest, the fractional loss of energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></msub></math>. The values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></msub></math> are respectively
question: It is found that if a neutron suffers an elastic collinear collision with a deuterium at rest, the fractional loss of its energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></msub></math>, while for its similar collision with a carbon nucleus at rest, the fractional loss of energy is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></msub></math>. The values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></msub></math> are respectively
solution:
Since, the linear momentum is conserved, u=V1+2V2.

e=1=V2-V1u (where e⇒the coefficient of restitution), u=V2-V1

V2=2u3 ;V1=-u3. Initial energy, =12u2. Final energy, =12×1×u29=u218. Fractional change =u22-u218u22=0.88



u=V1+12V2⇒1=V2-V1u⇒u=V2-V1
⇒V2=2u13⇒V1=-11u13.
Change in energy,=u2-11u132×100=0.294

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body of mass 14 kg initially at rest explodes and breaks into three fragments of masses in the ratio $2: 2: 3$. The two pieces of equal masses fly off perpendicular to each other with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ each. The velocity of the heavier fragment is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
question: A body of mass 14 kg initially at rest explodes and breaks into three fragments of masses in the ratio $2: 2: 3$. The two pieces of equal masses fly off perpendicular to each other with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ each. The velocity of the heavier fragment is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
From mass ratio 2:2:3 with total mass 14 kg: individual masses are 4 kg, 4 kg, and 6 kg.
Setting coordinate axes along the motion directions of the two equal fragments: fragment 1 has momentum $\vec{p_1} = 4 \times 18\hat{i} = 72\hat{i}$ kg⋅m/s, and fragment 2 has $\vec{p_2} = 4 \times 18\hat{j} = 72\hat{j}$ kg⋅m/s.
By momentum conservation, initially at rest: $\vec{p_1} + \vec{p_2} + \vec{p_3} = 0$, so $\vec{p_3} = -72\hat{i} - 72\hat{j}$ kg⋅m/s.
Velocity of 6 kg fragment: $\vec{v_3} = \frac{\vec{p_3}}{6} = -12\hat{i} - 12\hat{j}$ m/s.
Speed: $|\vec{v_3}| = \sqrt{144 + 144} = \sqrt{288} = 12\sqrt{2}$ m/s.

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A particle of mass m is moving along the x-axis with initial velocity ui^. It collides elastically with a particle of mass 10m at rest and then moves with half its initial kinetic energy (see figure). If  sin θ1=nsin θ2 then value of n is ________.
question: A particle of mass m is moving along the x-axis with initial velocity ui^. It collides elastically with a particle of mass 10m at rest and then moves with half its initial kinetic energy (see figure). If  sin θ1=nsin θ2 then value of n is ________.
solution:
From momentum conservation in perpendicular direction of initial motion.
mv1sinθ1=10mv2sinθ2
Given that 12mu212=12mv12  …….(2)
From equation (1) & (2)
sinθ1=10sinθ2
n=10

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A simple pendulum of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> has a wooden bob of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. It is struck by a bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The bullet gets embedded into the bob. The height to which the bob rises before swinging back is. (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: A simple pendulum of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> has a wooden bob of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>. It is struck by a bullet of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> moving with a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The bullet gets embedded into the bob. The height to which the bob rises before swinging back is. (use <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Linear momentum will be conserved, therefore

mu=M+mV

⇒10−2×2×102≅1×V

⇒V≅2 m s-1

Again from energy conservation, we get height achieved, h=V22g=0.2m.

q: As shown in figure. When a spherical cavity (centred at O ) of radius 1 is cut out of a uniform sphere of radius R (centred at C ), the centre of mass of remaining (shaded part of sphere is at G, i.e., on the surface of the cavity. R can be determined by the equation:
question: As shown in figure. When a spherical cavity (centred at O ) of radius 1 is cut out of a uniform sphere of radius R (centred at C ), the centre of mass of remaining (shaded part of sphere is at G, i.e., on the surface of the cavity. R can be determined by the equation:
solution:
M1=43πR3ρ
M2=43π13-ρ
Xcom=M1X1+M2X2M1+M2
⇒43πR3ρ0+43π13-ρR-143πR3ρ+43π13-ρ-2-R
⇒R-1(R3-1)=2-RR≠1
R-1R-1R2+R+1=2-1
R2+R+12-R=1
Alternative:
Mremaining2-R=Mcavity1-R
⇒R3-132-R=13R-1
⇒R2+R+12-R=1

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: An alpha- particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> suffers <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">-</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi></math> dimensional elastic collision with a nucleus at rest of unknown mass. It is scattered directly backwards losing <math><mn>64</mn><mi>%</mi></math> of its initial kinetic energy. The mass of the nucleus is
question: An alpha- particle of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> suffers <math><mn>1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">-</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi></math> dimensional elastic collision with a nucleus at rest of unknown mass. It is scattered directly backwards losing <math><mn>64</mn><mi>%</mi></math> of its initial kinetic energy. The mass of the nucleus is
solution:
From momentum conservation,

mv+0=-mv1+Mv2   ...(1) 

Using kinetic energy conservation,

12mv2=12mv12+12Mv22⇒12mv2=0.36×12mv2+12Mv22⇒0.64mv2=Mv22⇒v2=0.8vmM 

As it retain 36%,⇒0.36×12mv2=12mv12 ⇒0.36v2=v12 Hence, v1=0.6v
From (1), mv=-m0.6v+M0.8vmM

⇒1=-0.6+0.8Mm

On solving, we get M=4m

q:
A balloon and its content having mass $M$ is moving up with an acceleration ' $a$ '. The mass that must be released from the content so that the balloon starts moving up with an acceleration ' $3 a^{\prime}$ will be
(Take ' g ' as acceleration due to gravity)
question:
A balloon and its content having mass $M$ is moving up with an acceleration ' $a$ '. The mass that must be released from the content so that the balloon starts moving up with an acceleration ' $3 a^{\prime}$ will be
(Take ' g ' as acceleration due to gravity)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & F-m g=m a \\ & F=m a+m g \\ & F-(m-x) g=(m-x) 3 a \end{aligned}$ Put F $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Ma}+\mathrm{mg}-\mathrm{mg}+\mathrm{xg}=3 \mathrm{ma}-3 \mathrm{xa} \\ & \mathrm{x}=\frac{2 \mathrm{ma}}{\mathrm{~g}+3 \mathrm{a}} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A body $A$ of mass $M$ while falling vertically downwards under gravity breaks into two parts; a body B of mass $1 / 3 \mathrm{M}$ and a body $\mathrm{C}$ of mass $2 / 3 \mathrm{M}$. The centre of mass of bodies $\mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ taken together shifts compared to that of body $\mathrm{A}$ towards
question: A body $A$ of mass $M$ while falling vertically downwards under gravity breaks into two parts; a body B of mass $1 / 3 \mathrm{M}$ and a body $\mathrm{C}$ of mass $2 / 3 \mathrm{M}$. The centre of mass of bodies $\mathrm{B}$ and $\mathrm{C}$ taken together shifts compared to that of body $\mathrm{A}$ towards
solution:
No horizontal external force is acting
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{cm}}=0 \\
& \text { since } \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{cm}}=0 \\
& \therefore \Delta \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{cm}}=0
\end{aligned}
$$

q: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> with an initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> collides perfectly elastically with a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> at rest. It moves with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> after collision, then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></math> is given by
question: A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> with an initial velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi><mover><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> collides perfectly elastically with a mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> at rest. It moves with a velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mover><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></math> after collision, then, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi></math> is given by
solution:
From momentum conservation

mui^+0=mvj^+3mv→'

v→'=u3i^-v3j^

From kinetic energy conservation 12mu2=12mv2+12(3m)u32+v32

solving v=u2

q: The position of the centre of mass of a uniform semi-circular wire of radius R placed in x-y plane with its centre at the origin and the line joining its ends as x-axis is given by, 0, xRπ. Then, the value of |x| is          .
question: The position of the centre of mass of a uniform semi-circular wire of radius R placed in x-y plane with its centre at the origin and the line joining its ends as x-axis is given by, 0, xRπ. Then, the value of |x| is          .
solution:
COM of semi-circular ring is at 2Rπ

Distance from centre ⇒x=2

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A simple pendulum, made of a string of length <math><mi>l</mi></math> and <math><mi>a</mi></math> bob of mass <math><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is released from a small angle <math><msub><mrow><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> It strikes a block of mass <math><mi>M</mi><mo>,</mo></math> kept on horizontal surface at its lowest point of oscillations, elastically. It bounces back and goes up to an angle <math><msub><mrow><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math><mi>M</mi></math> is given by:
question: A simple pendulum, made of a string of length <math><mi>l</mi></math> and <math><mi>a</mi></math> bob of mass <math><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is released from a small angle <math><msub><mrow><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> It strikes a block of mass <math><mi>M</mi><mo>,</mo></math> kept on horizontal surface at its lowest point of oscillations, elastically. It bounces back and goes up to an angle <math><msub><mrow><mi>θ</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math><mi>M</mi></math> is given by:
solution:
u=2 gl1-cos θ0 .......(i)

v= velocity of ball after collision

v=m-Mm+Mu

Since ball rises up to angle θ1

v=2gl1-cos θ1=m-Mm+Mu ......(ii)

From (i) and (ii)

m-Mm+M=1-cos θ11-cos θ0=sinθ12sinθ02

⇒Mm=θ0-θ1θ0+θ1⇒M=θ0-θ1θ0+θ1m

chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: Three identical spheres each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> are placed at the corners of a right angled triangle with mutually perpendicular sides equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> each. Taking point of intersection of mutually perpendicular sides as origin, the magnitude of position vector of centre of mass of the system will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
question: Three identical spheres each of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> are placed at the corners of a right angled triangle with mutually perpendicular sides equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> each. Taking point of intersection of mutually perpendicular sides as origin, the magnitude of position vector of centre of mass of the system will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
The diagram represents the locations of the masses as mentioned in the question.

For point 1, r1→=0i^+0j^ for point 2, r2→=3i^+0j^ and for point 3, r3→=0i^+3j^.

The formula for centre of mass is

r→com=m1r1→+m2r2→+m3r3→m1+m2+m3

⇒r→com=M0i^+0j^+M3i^+M3j^3M

⇒r→com=i^+j^

r→com=12+12=2=x

⇒x=2

⇒  yx=1+32=1.37

subject: Physics
chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
q: A thin rod of mass 0.9 kg and length 1 m is suspended, at rest, from one end so that it can freely oscillate in the vertical plane. A particle of move 0.1 kg moving in a straight line with velocity 80 m s-1 hits the rod at its bottom most point and sticks to it (see figure). The angular speed (in rad s-1) of the rod immediately after the collision will be …………
question: A thin rod of mass 0.9 kg and length 1 m is suspended, at rest, from one end so that it can freely oscillate in the vertical plane. A particle of move 0.1 kg moving in a straight line with velocity 80 m s-1 hits the rod at its bottom most point and sticks to it (see figure). The angular speed (in rad s-1) of the rod immediately after the collision will be …………
solution:
Given,

Mass of the rod, M=0.9 kg

Mass of particle, m=0.1 kg

Length of the rod, l=1 m

Velocity of particle, v=80 m s-1

No external torque acting on the system of rod and particle, so angular momentum of system about the suspension is conserved, i.e.; Li=Lf

⇒mvl=Iω  ...(1)

Where, I is the momentum of inertia of rod and particle system, ω is the angular velocity of system just after the collision.

⇒mvl=Ml23+ml2ω  ...(2)

Substitute the values in equation (2)

⇒0.1×80×1=0.9×(1)23+0.1×(1)2×ω

ω=20 rad s-1
